name: Juwayriya bint al-Harith
title: Juwayriya bint al-Harith (RA): A Blessing to Her People
meta_description: The story of Juwayriya bint al-Harith (RA), the captive who became a wife of the Prophet Muhammad and a blessing to her whole tribe. Her dignity, her worship, and the gift of dhikr she left us.
keywords: Juwayriya bint al-Harith, story of Juwayriya, Banu Mustaliq, wife of Prophet Muhammad, mothers of the believers, sahaba stories, dhikr
lede: She walked into Madinah as a captive, the daughter of a defeated chief, her husband killed and her father fled. She had nothing left: no standing, no freedom, no one to turn to. By the end of that week her entire people had been set free, and she was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.
This is the story of Juwayriya bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her): the woman of whom it was said that no one was ever a greater blessing to her people than she was to hers.

paras: In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, one of the ways hostilities between two peoples were ended was through marriage. A leader would marry a woman of standing from a rival tribe, and the bond of family would soften what war had hardened. Three of the marriages of the Prophet ﷺ carry this kind of wisdom, each reaching toward a people who had stood against him. Juwayriya was one of them, and of all three, the blessing that came from her marriage was the most visible.

She came from Banu Mustaliq, a powerful tribe that lived outside Makkah, near a well-known body of water between Makkah and Madinah, and was tied to Quraysh by blood. Her father, al-Harith, was their chief. By the standing of her people she was, in everything but the name, a princess.

Her tribe had stayed out of the early conflicts of Makkah. But after the Muslims migrated to Madinah, the caravans that passed through their lands became a matter of interest, and Banu Mustaliq saw reason to side with Quraysh. Quraysh, for their part, urged them on: the Muslims would not expect an attack from a tribe with no quarrel against them, and the spoils, they were told, would be great.
Her name then was not Juwayriya. It was Barrah, a name that meant something close to righteous. The Prophet ﷺ would later change it, and that small change carries a lesson of its own.

sections.h2: A plot, and a careful answer to it
paras: When word reached the Prophet ﷺ that Banu Mustaliq were preparing to strike, he did not act on the rumour. He sent a man named Buraydah, a bedouin who had embraced Islam during the hijra, to go quietly among them and confirm the truth.

Buraydah went as if he were a fighter looking to join them for a share of the spoils. He fit the part: an unknown bedouin from the open desert, a face no one in Makkah or among Banu Mustaliq would recognise. Al-Harith confirmed the plan to him in full, even naming when it would happen. Only then, with the threat verified, did the Prophet ﷺ set out to stop the attack before it could happen.

There is a quiet justice in this. He would not march against a people on hearsay. He confirmed first, and only then did he move. The army reached the tribe's water and rained arrows down upon them until they surrendered, a siege that spared lives rather than spilling them. Fewer than ten were killed among them, and only one among the Muslims, struck by mistake. The idol the tribe had worshipped was destroyed, and a great deal of livestock and wealth was taken, along with many captives.

sections.h2: The captive who asked to be free
paras: Juwayriya's husband was among those killed. Her father escaped. She herself was taken captive, the most royal of all the prisoners brought back to Madinah, and she fell to the share of Thabit ibn Qays.

She did not sit in silence. She invoked a contract of ransom: a fixed price by which a captive could buy back their own freedom. The sum agreed was something she could realistically gather or have paid on her behalf. She had a way out, and she took it into her own hands.

Aisha (RA), who tells much of this story, said Juwayriya was a woman of striking beauty and elegance. No one met her and thought her ordinary. So when she came knocking, seeking help with her ransom, Aisha admitted that her own heart tightened at the sight of her.

Juwayriya came to the Prophet ﷺ and told him plainly who she was: the daughter of a chief, struck by this tragedy, now bound by a ransom she could not easily pay. She had come to ask him to settle it.

He could have done exactly that, paid the ransom and let her go. He was willing to. But he offered her something else. "Would you like something better than that?" he asked. "I will pay your ransom, and I will marry you." She said yes.

When the news spread that the Prophet ﷺ was marrying the daughter of al-Harith, the Muslims looked at the prisoners in their hands and could not keep them. "These are now the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ," they said, and they set them free. Over a hundred families of Banu Mustaliq walked out of captivity because of her.
It is from here that Aisha said her famous words: she knew of no woman who had ever been a greater blessing to her people than Juwayriya was to hers.

sections.h2: Her father, and the two hidden camels
paras: Al-Harith, not yet knowing what had happened, gathered all the wealth he had left to ransom his daughter and set out for Madinah. On the way, passing through a valley near the city, he saw two camels he loved too much to part with, and he hid them.
He came before the Prophet ﷺ and offered everything else as her ransom. The Prophet ﷺ smiled and asked him, "Where are the two camels you hid in the valley?" and described the exact spot.

Al-Harith stood there stunned. "No one knew about those camels except Allah," he said. "I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that you are the Messenger of Allah." He accepted Islam in that moment, and his two sons with him. With that, the whole of Banu Mustaliq came into Islam: the people who had set out to attack Madinah were now its allies.

sections.h2: A new name, and a new heart
paras: The Prophet ﷺ changed her name from Barrah to Juwayriya. He disliked names that sounded like a person was praising their own righteousness, and he did not want it said that he had left a house called righteousness behind him. The lesson is gentle: do not announce your own piety, live it.

And live it she did. Whatever this world had once meant to the daughter of a chief, it fell away. Food that came into her home left it again almost at once, given away. One day the Prophet ﷺ came home and asked if there was anything to eat, and the only thing in the house was a single bone that someone had gifted them.

She became known above all for two things: her fasting, and her remembrance of Allah. She loved to fast so much that it is from her we learn a gentle ruling. The Prophet ﷺ found her fasting on a Friday and asked whether she had fasted the day before, or planned to fast the day after. When she said no to both, he told her to break her fast, for that day is not to be singled out alone.

She would spend long hours in a small corner of her home that she kept for prayer, sitting in dhikr. It is around this quiet devotion that one of the most beautiful gifts in our worship was given to us.

sections.h2: Four words that outweigh the morning
paras: One morning the Prophet ﷺ left her sitting in her prayer corner, deep in remembrance. He returned hours later and found her exactly as he had left her, still making dhikr.

He told her he would teach her words that, said only three times, would outweigh all the remembrance she had been making since dawn: "Glory be to Allah and praise be to Him, as many as the number of His creation, as much as pleases Him, as much as the weight of His Throne, and as much as the ink of His words."

It is a remembrance that countless Muslims still say every morning and evening. The one who guides others to a good deed shares in its reward, and so the reward of every soul who has ever said these words returns, in part, to Juwayriya. From a single morning in a small corner, a gift to the whole community.

Because she had entered his life as a captive, she was sometimes looked down upon. When one word wounded her, she came to the Prophet ﷺ, and he found a way to lift her: the ransom paid for her had been higher than the gift given to any of his wives. She left comforted. It was a kindness he showed her more than once.

She kept far from every conflict that came after the Prophet ﷺ passed away, living quietly in worship until her death, fifty-six years after the hijra. She is, like all the Mothers of the Believers, a wife of the Prophet ﷺ in Paradise.

lessons_intro: Her story turns on a single choice the Prophet ﷺ made, and on what she became once mercy reached her. Both halves are worth carrying.

lessons.bold: Mercy can undo what force cannot.
lessons.text: One marriage, offered out of kindness, freed a hundred families and turned an enemy tribe into allies. Gentleness reached further than any victory.

lessons.bold: Do not advertise your own goodness.
lessons.text: He changed her name away from one that sounded like self-praise. Real righteousness is lived quietly, not announced.

lessons.bold: Let this world pass through your hands.
lessons.text: A chief's daughter gave away her food the moment it arrived and kept almost nothing. Her wealth became the unseen kind.
From one morning of dhikr came a gift recited by millions. What you do alone with Allah can outlast you by centuries.

faq.a: She was the daughter of al-Harith, the chief of the tribe of Banu Mustaliq, and she became a wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She is remembered for her worship and as a blessing to her people.

faq.q: How did Juwayriya come to marry the Prophet?
faq.a: She was taken captive after the battle with Banu Mustaliq and sought help to pay her ransom. The Prophet ﷺ offered to pay it and to marry her, and she accepted. When the Muslims saw that her people were now his in-laws, they freed all their captives.

Her name was Barrah, which suggested righteousness. The Prophet ﷺ changed it to Juwayriya because he disliked names that sounded like self-praise and did not want it said that he had left a house of "righteousness" behind.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Juwayriya?
faq.a: That mercy can change more than force, that goodness is meant to be lived rather than announced, and that quiet remembrance of Allah is never small. The morning dhikr taught to her is still recited today.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Juwayriya entered Madinah with nothing: no husband, no freedom, no standing left. What she did with what came next is the part worth keeping. She asked for her freedom instead of waiting for it. She let the world pass through her hands. She gave her best hours to a quiet corner and the remembrance of Allah, and out of that corner came a gift the whole community still carries.

Most of our lives are lived far from any battle or great event, in small rooms, with ordinary days. Her life says those rooms are enough. She was scorned for where she had come from, and she answered it not with bitterness but with worship. Keep a corner for your Lord, hold this world loosely, let no one's words decide your worth, and trust that what you do quietly with Allah is never wasted. Carry one of those into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Juwayriya bint al-Harith (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

social.caption:
She entered Madinah as a captive: the daughter of a defeated chief, her husband killed, her father fled. By the end of that week, her entire tribe had been set free, and she was a wife of the Prophet ﷺ.

This is Juwayriya bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her). When the Prophet ﷺ married her, the Muslims freed all their prisoners, saying they could not keep his in-laws captive. Aisha (RA) said she knew of no woman who was ever a greater blessing to her people.

She spent her life in fasting and remembrance, and the morning dhikr the Prophet ﷺ taught her is recited to this day.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/juwayriya-bint-al-harith

email.subject: The captive who became a blessing to her whole people
email.alt_subject: "Would you like something better than that?"
email.preview: She entered Madinah with nothing. By the end of the week, her entire tribe was free.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

She entered Madinah as a captive. Her husband had been killed, her father had fled, and she was the daughter of a defeated chief with nothing left. By the end of that week, her entire tribe had been set free.

This is the story of Juwayriya bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her).

When the Prophet ﷺ first heard that her people were planning to attack the Muslims, he did not act on the rumour. He sent a man to confirm it, and only then did he move to stop them. There is a quiet justice in that detail alone.

After the battle, Juwayriya was taken captive. She did not wait in silence. She invoked a contract of ransom and came to the Prophet ﷺ to ask for help paying it. He offered her more than she asked for. "Would you like something better than that?" he said. "I will pay your ransom, and I will marry you."

When the news spread, the Muslims looked at the prisoners they held and could not keep them. "These are now the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ," they said, and they freed them all. Over a hundred families walked out of captivity because of her. Aisha (RA) said she knew of no woman who had ever been a greater blessing to her people.

What she became afterward is the part I keep returning to. A chief's daughter, she gave her food away the moment it came into her home. She kept a small corner for prayer and spent long hours there in the remembrance of Allah. One morning the Prophet ﷺ left her in dhikr and returned hours later to find her exactly where she had been.

He taught her then a few words that, said three times, would outweigh a whole morning of remembrance. Muslims still say them every day. The reward of every one of them returns, in part, to her.

There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss: what you do quietly with Allah is never small. From one morning in one corner came a gift the whole community still carries.

May Allah be pleased with her.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: She walked into Madinah as a captive: the daughter of a defeated chief, her husband killed in the battle, her father fled. She had no standing left and no freedom. By the end of that week, her whole people would be free, and she would be a wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

Juwayriya bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her) came from Banu Mustaliq, a powerful tribe that had set out to attack the Muslims. When the Prophet ﷺ first heard of the plan, he refused to act on a rumour. He sent a man to confirm the truth, and only then did he move to stop them.

After the battle, Juwayriya was taken among the prisoners. She did not sit in silence. She invoked a contract of ransom and came to the Prophet ﷺ to ask for help paying it. He offered her something more. "Would you like something better than that?" he said. "I will pay your ransom, and I will marry you." She said yes.

When the news spread that he was marrying the daughter of their chief, the Muslims could not keep their prisoners. "These are now the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ," they said, and over a hundred families walked free. Aisha (RA) said she knew of no woman who had ever been a greater blessing to her people.

Her father came to ransom her, hiding two beloved camels in a valley on the way. "Where are the two camels you hid?" the Prophet ﷺ asked him, and named the very spot. "No one knew about them except Allah," the man said, and he accepted Islam on the spot, and his sons with him.

Whatever the world had once meant to her fell away. She gave her food away the moment it arrived, until one day the only thing in the house was a single bone. She kept a small corner for prayer and filled it with the remembrance of Allah.

One morning the Prophet ﷺ left her in dhikr and returned hours later to find her exactly as he had left her. He taught her then a few words that, said three times, would outweigh her whole morning of remembrance. Muslims say them still. From one quiet corner came a gift the whole community carries.
storybook.pullquote: I know of no woman who was a greater blessing to her people than she was.
storybook.reflection: Juwayriya's life turns on mercy received and worship given. A single act of kindness from the Prophet ﷺ freed her whole tribe, and a single quiet corner of her home gave the community a remembrance it still recites. Her story says that what we do gently, and what we do alone with Allah, can reach further than anything loud. Keep a corner for your Lord; nothing done there is ever wasted.
